name: Austria
overview: PhD candidates are hired as university assistants under the collective agreement. Minimum gross salary is ~€3,277/month gross (based on 30 hours/week, approx. €2,300 net/month). STEM contracts are often topped up to 40 hours/week (~€3,050 net/month). Includes 14 monthly salary payouts per year.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via FWF (Austrian Science Fund)
Full employee status with 14 salary payouts per year.
Strong academic networks and high quality of life.
cons: Rents are rising in Vienna and Innsbruck.
Requires teaching contributions in local languages.

name: Belgium
overview: Predoctoral researchers are typically funded via tax-free doctoral fellowships (stipendiat) of €2,200 to €2.450/month net. It is tax-free but covers basic health and social security insurance. Rents in Brussels, Ghent, and Leuven are moderate-high.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via FWO (Flanders)
High tax-free net stipend with basic health insurance.
Excellent research groups and central European location.
cons: No standard pension contributions during the fellowship.
Complex regional university administrations (Flanders vs. Wallonia).

name: Bulgaria
overview: Bulgaria provides state-funded PhD candidates with a flat monthly stipend of ~1,000 BGN (approx. €510 net). Living expenses in Sofia are low.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via Ministry of Education and Science
Very low cost of daily living and services.
Cheap student dormitory options.
cons: Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
Lowest base PhD salary in the EU.
Heavy administrative requirements and high language barrier.

name: Croatia
overview: Croatia employs PhD researchers on assistant contracts, often funded by the Croatian Science Foundation (HRZZ). The gross salary is ~€1,400/month, translating to a net pay of ~€1,050 to €1,150/month with full health and social security benefits.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Vibrant Mediterranean lifestyle & sunny weather
Excellent funding via HRZZ (Croatian Science Foundation)
Full employee rights, including health cover and pension accrual.
Low cost of living in historical coastal/academic cities.
cons: Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
Low salary scale relative to Western European averages.
Highly document-heavy public administration and language barrier.

name: Cyprus
overview: Cyprus employs predoctoral researchers primarily on European-funded research grants (Horizon Europe). Typical monthly net salaries range from €1,200 to €1,500.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Vibrant Mediterranean lifestyle & sunny weather
Excellent funding via Research and Innovation Foundation of Cyprus
Vibrant, English-friendly Mediterranean academic settings.
High funding levels in specific EU-sponsored research institutes.
cons: Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
Limited local tenure opportunities post-PhD.
Car-dependent infrastructure outside city centers.

name: Czech Republic
overview: PhD candidates in the Czech Republic receive a state stipend (~11,250 CZK/month tax-free, approx. €450 net/month), which is frequently topped up by university or project employment to ~€1,200/month net.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via GACR (Czech Science Foundation)
Low tuition fees and very affordable dormitory housing.
Prague is a highly attractive, central European city.
cons: Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
Base state stipend is too low without university top-ups.
Strict predoctoral milestones before completing studies.

name: Denmark
overview: Salaried employee contracts with high starting salaries (~€2,500 to €3,000/month net) and a generous 17.1% university-paid pension scheme.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via Independent Research Fund Denmark
High net starting salaries and excellent pension schemes.
Very flat hierarchy and high work autonomy.
cons: Severe housing shortage & high rental costs
High personal income taxes.
Very high cost of living and housing shortages in Copenhagen.
warnings: High daily cost of living in university towns

name: Estonia
overview: Estonia employs PhD candidates as junior researchers, providing standard employee rights. Junior researchers receive a guaranteed salary aligned with the national average wage (approx. €1,750 to €1,900 gross/month, yielding ~€1,400 to €1,550 net/month).
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via Estonian Research Council
Highly digitalized, paperless university administration.
Full employee status with standard health cover and pension.
cons: Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Winters are long, dark, and cold.
Relatively high rental costs in Tallinn and Tartu.

name: Finland
overview: PhD researchers are hired on university contracts starting at ~€2,200 gross/month (~€1,650 net) up to €3,000 gross/month. Covers health insurance and public pension accumulation.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via Academy of Finland
Salaried contract with full pension and social security.
Excellent work-life balance and pristine nature.
cons: Severe housing shortage & high rental costs
Cold, dark winters.
High tax rates on salaries.

name: France
overview: PhD candidates are typically employed on doctoral contracts (contrat doctoral). Standard minimum gross salary is ~€2,100/month gross, resulting in a net take-home salary of ~€1,650/month. Covers basic health, unemployment, and pension insurance.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Vibrant Mediterranean lifestyle & sunny weather
Excellent funding via Ministry of Higher Education and Research (contrat doctoral)
Standardized employee contract with full worker rights.
Low tuition fees at public universities.
cons: Complex administration bureaucracy (often in French only).
High rents in Paris and large university cities.

name: Germany
overview: PhD contracts in Germany are usually TV-L E13 employee contracts. Around 75% of STEM positions are funded at the 65% to 75% scale (~€1,950–€2,250 net/month), and some (like Computer Science/Engineering) are funded at 100% (~€2,700–€2,900 net/month). Humanities/Social Sciences are often on 50% scale (~€1,450–€1,600 net/month) but still work full-time.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via DFG (German Research Foundation)
Generous TV-L E13 employee rights & public pension accrual.
Highly integrated industrial and research networks.
cons: Heavy regional administration and bureaucracy.
Humanities often receive low contract percentages (50%).

name: Greece
overview: Greece funds PhD candidates through tax-free stipends from the State Scholarship Foundation (IKY) or ELIDEK, typically averaging €900 to €1,100/month net.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Vibrant Mediterranean lifestyle & sunny weather
Excellent funding via IKY (State Scholarships Foundation)
Low cost of daily living and dining.
Tax-free stipend status.
cons: Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
Very low stipend compared to Western Europe.
Limited laboratory infrastructure and slow university bureaucracy.

name: Hungary
overview: Hungary offers a national predoctoral scholarship: before the complex exam (years 1 & 2) the tax-free stipend is 140,000 HUF/month (~€350/mo net), which increases to 180,000 HUF/month (~€460/mo net) for years 3 & 4.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via Hungarian Doctoral Council
Universal tax-free stipend for all state-funded slots.
Extremely cheap university dormitory options.
cons: Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
Stipend is very low and requires additional teaching or part-time work to survive.
Strict academic requirements for passing the complex exam at 2 years.

name: Ireland
overview: Funded primarily through Irish Research Council (IRC) awards or university fellowships. The standard minimum stipend is ~€22,000 tax-free per year (~€1,833/month net). Grant holders do not pay income tax, and tuition fees are covered separately.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via Irish Research Council (IRC)
Stipend is completely tax-free.
Excellent tech and biotech post-PhD industry connections.
cons: Severe housing shortage & high rental costs
No pension contributions built up during studies
Dublin rental costs can exceed the entire stipend.
No pension contributions or standard employee contract protections.
warnings: High daily cost of living in university towns

name: Italy
overview: Italy relies on national doctoral scholarships (borsa di dottorato) of €16,243 gross/year. After INPS Gestione Separata contributions (approx. 33% shared/individual), candidates receive around €1,180 to €1,200/month net. This scholarship is tax-free but lacks pension accrual.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Basic social security coverage
Vibrant Mediterranean lifestyle & sunny weather
Excellent funding via MUR (Ministry of University and Research)
Doctoral scholarship is completely tax-free.
Reasonable cost of living in southern and central regions.
cons: Net stipend is very low for northern cities like Milan/Turin.
Highly document-heavy public administration bureaucracy.

name: Latvia
overview: Latvia supports PhD candidates through state stipends (~€250/month tax-free) supplemented by university or European research contract employment. This typically results in a combined net take-home salary of €800 to €950/month.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via Latvian Council of Science
Low cost of living and affordable student accommodation.
Beautiful nature and Baltic coastal access.
cons: Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
Base state stipend alone is not survivable.
Limited funding for humanities and social sciences.

name: Lithuania
overview: Lithuania provides tax-free government scholarships for doctoral students: €930/month in the 1st year and €1,075/month for years 2 to 4. Rents in Vilnius are moderate.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via Research Council of Lithuania
Scholarship is completely tax-free.
Low cost of living and modern digital infrastructure.
cons: Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
No standard pension accrual or employee contract rights.
Language barrier in municipal and off-campus offices.

name: Luxembourg
overview: Luxembourg hires doctoral candidates on standard university employment contracts (often funded by AFR). Gross salaries are €3,100 to €3,400/month, yielding a net pay of ~€2,500 to €2,750/month.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via FNR (Luxembourg National Research Fund)
Very high net salary (approx. €2,600 net) with full pension.
Highly international, multilingual research labs.
cons: Severe housing shortage & high rental costs
Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Rents are extremely high and finding housing is difficult.
Limited local student life outside the university town of Belval.
warnings: High daily cost of living in university towns

name: Malta
overview: Malta funds PhD candidates via University of Malta or MGSS scholarships, providing €8,500 to €11,000/year tax-free (~€700–€900/month net).
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Vibrant Mediterranean lifestyle & sunny weather
Excellent funding via Ministry for Education Malta
English is an official language, making integration immediate.
Tax-free stipend status.
cons: Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
Stipend is low relative to rising rental costs in expat hubs.
Limited research funding for large lab materials.

name: Netherlands
overview: Fully salaried employee status governed by the Dutch CAO-NU. Salary scales start at scale P1 (~€2,770 gross/mo, ~€2,200 net/mo) and scale up to scale P4 in year 4 (~€3,530 gross/mo, ~€2,750 net/mo). Includes 8% holiday allowance, 8.3% year-end bonus, and full pension accumulation.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via NWO (Dutch Research Council)
Highest standard employee salaries and holiday/end-year bonuses.
Outstanding university support networks and high English proficiency.
cons: Severe housing shortage & high rental costs
Severe national housing shortage with high rent.
High cost of daily living and services in academic hubs.
warnings: High daily cost of living in university towns

name: Norway
overview: PhD candidates are hired as public servants (Stipendiat, code 1017). Starting salaries begin at scale 54, yielding ~532,000 NOK gross/year (~44,300 NOK gross/month, resulting in ~30,000 to 32,000 NOK/mo net, approx. €2,750 net/month). Enjoy full employee benefits and state pension accrual.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent work-life balance & equality focus
Excellent funding via Research Council of Norway
Excellent starting salary with full public servant benefits.
Exceptional work-life balance and generous parental leave.
cons: Severe housing shortage & high rental costs
Very high cost of living, especially for services and eating out.
Highly competitive application process.
warnings: High daily cost of living in university towns

name: Poland
overview: Poland utilizes a two-tier stipend structure for Doctoral Schools. In the first two years (before mid-term evaluation), candidates receive a lower tier of ~3,467 PLN gross/month (~€800 gross, ~€720 net after pension/social security deductions). After a successful mid-term evaluation (next two years), the stipend increases to ~5,340 PLN gross/month (~€1,240 gross, ~€1,100 net). Stipends are tax-free but subject to social security pension deductions.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Basic social security coverage
Excellent funding via NCN (National Science Centre Poland)
Stipend increases significantly after the 2-year mid-term evaluation.
No tuition fees for doctoral school programs.
cons: Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
Stipend in the first two years is tight for renting in Warsaw.
Subject to social security pension deductions since 2019 reform.

name: Portugal
overview: PhD candidates in Portugal typically receive standard funding and research access.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Vibrant Mediterranean lifestyle & sunny weather
Excellent funding via FCT (Foundation for Science and Technology)
Tax-free or structured funding options.
cons: No pension contributions built up during studies
Cost of living or stay-back complexities depending on nationality.

name: Romania
overview: Romania provides ministry stipends of ~1,800 RON/month (~€360/mo net) for the first two years, increasing to ~2,200 RON/month (~€440/mo net) in years 3 & 4. Frequently topped up by research grants to €600–€800/month net.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via UEFISCDI Romania
Low cost of daily living and housing.
Fast-growing IT and tech hubs in Cluj and Bucharest.
cons: Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
Base state stipend is very low.
Heavy reliance on project-based top-ups which vary between labs.

name: Slovakia
overview: Slovakia offers standardized junior researcher scholarships: €1,025/month tax-free before passing the dissertation exam, and €1,195/month tax-free after passing the exam.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via Slovak Ministry of Education
Significant stipend increase after the dissertation exam.
Tax-free stipend status with health insurance included.
cons: Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Low starting stipend compared to Northern Europe
No pension contributions built up during studies
No standard employee social benefits or pension accrual.
Slow academic administrative processes.

name: Slovenia
overview: Slovenia employs doctoral researchers under the 'Young Researcher' (Mladi raziskovalec) program. The gross salary is €1,650 to €1,850/month, resulting in a net take-home pay of ~€1,150 to €1,280/month.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via Slovenian Research Agency (ARRS)
Full employee status with pension, travel, and lunch allowances.
Clean, safe, and beautiful alpine nature.
cons: Limited number of Young Researcher slots available annually.
Language barrier for teaching and local administration.

name: Spain
overview: Predoctoral contracts (contrato predoctoral) are regulated by the EPIF statute. Salaries scale as percentages of standard categories: 56% in years 1-2 (~€18,000/yr gross, ~€1,250 net/mo), 60% in year 3 (~€19,000/yr gross, ~€1,350 net/mo), and 75% in year 4 (~€23,000/yr gross, ~€1,650 net/mo). Cover health insurance and full employment rights.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Vibrant Mediterranean lifestyle & sunny weather
Excellent funding via FPU (University Teacher Training) fellowships
Predoctoral contract provides solid legal employee rights.
Vibrant academic communities and outstanding quality of life.
cons: Stipend is very low relative to Madrid and Barcelona rental costs.
Varying research budgets between institutions.

name: Sweden
overview: Salaried employee status (Doktorandtjänst) with full social benefits, governed by strong collective bargaining agreements. Net salaries start at ~€2,000 to €2,400/month with annual increases.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via Swedish Research Council (Vetenskapsrådet)
Clear pathway to permanent residency after 4 years.
Outstanding work-life balance and parental leave benefits.
cons: Long, dark, and cold winters.
High tax rates and long queues for public rentals.

name: Switzerland
overview: Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) salaries start at CHF 48,000 gross/year in year 1 (~CHF 4,000/mo gross, ~CHF 3,300/mo net) up to CHF 50,000 in year 4. Major institutions (ETH Zurich, EPFL) offer higher scales up to CHF 85,000/year gross (~CHF 5,200/mo net). Social security contributions are automatically deducted, and tuition fees are low, but the cost of living is extremely high.
pros: Full employee rights & social benefits
State pension contributions covered
Excellent funding via SNSF (Swiss National Science Foundation)
Highest nominal PhD salaries in Europe (up to CHF 5,200/mo net).
World-renowned research labs and infrastructure (ETH/EPFL).
cons: Severe housing shortage & high rental costs
Strict visa stay-back terms post-PhD
Extremely high cost of living (rent, groceries, health insurance).
Strict immigration quotas for non-EU/EFTA graduates.
warnings: High daily cost of living in university towns

name: United Kingdom
overview: PhD funding is primarily distributed through tax-free UKRI minimum stipends. The standard rate is ~£19,237 tax-free per year (~£1,600/month net), with a London allowance top-up of £2,000 (~£1,765/month net). Since it is a tax-free stipend, it does not accumulate pension or national insurance benefits.
pros: Tax-free stipend income
Excellent funding via UKRI (UK Research and Innovation)
Stipend is 100% tax-free.
Opportunity for industry-sponsored CASE top-ups.
cons: Severe housing shortage & high rental costs
No pension contributions built up during studies
No social security benefits, paid parental leave, or pension accrual.
Dublin-equivalent severe rental crisis in major university cities.
